title: The Conclusion of the Matter
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 12-29-24 -AM- The Conclusion of the Matter - Galatians 5_16-6_1.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Ending the Year with the Fruit of the Spirit
subpoints: The 12th or 13th sermon in the Fruit of the Spirit series; concluding by looking at the passage as a whole — the fruit of the Spirit in the context of the grace of God
The two great enemies of the grace of God in our lives: legalism (obey these rules and then God will love you) and license (do what your flesh wants, grace will abound)
Both voices are loud, both are naturally attractive to the flesh — yet both are false; the Christian must listen instead to the voice of the Holy Spirit through his word: walk in the Spirit

outline.title: 1. What It Means to Walk in the Spirit (vv.16, 25)
subpoints: Five meanings: (1) you have received life from the Spirit — born again, born from above (Galatians 4:28-29); (2) you are controlled by the Spirit — Romans 8:1-2, 4-5, 9, 13; the one walking in the Spirit has his mind on the things of the Spirit; (3) you confess that Jesus is who the Bible says he is — 1 John 4:2; (4) the fruit of the Spirit is present in your life — Galatians 5:22-23; (5) you have a correct view of the law of God
The law of God should neither be blindly followed nor disregarded; both are sold as spiritual positions — but both are missing the Spirit

The law without the Spirit kills; the law is designed to open our eyes to see Christ; Romans 7 — what shall we say then, is the law sin? No; I would not have known sin except through the law; the law drives us to Christ

Once we trust Christ and nothing else for salvation, Jesus points us back to the law as the pattern to demonstrate our love for him — but this follows his love for us, not precedes it; John 14:15 — if you love me, keep my commandments; the love and faith come first, the keeping follows

outline.title: 2. The Works of the Flesh (vv.19-21)
subpoints: Paul says that those who unrepentantly, consistently, and without any struggle practice these things will not inherit the kingdom of God — not just commit them once, but practice them as a way of life without struggle

Five categories: sexually related sins (adultery, fornication, uncleanness, lewdness — including pornography and homosexuality, and the flaunting/approval of sin); eight relationship-destroying sins (hatred, contentions, jealousies, outbursts of wrath, selfish ambitions, dissensions, heresies in the sense of causing division to gain control, envy, murders); three God-displacing sins (idolatry, sorcery/witchcraft — the use of substances to alter the mind, the postmodern parallel being abortion and euthanasia; selfishness); two bodily sins (drunkenness, reveling/gluttony)
This list is not exhaustive — Paul adds "and the like"; 17 items in the NKJV; do not use the list to excuse your sin because it is not there, or to judge others whose sin is on the list
The solution for sin: repentance and walking in the Spirit — practically, making more room for Jesus

outline.title: 3. Walking in the Spirit Practically
subpoints: Read the Bible and listen to it preached with an act of listening, not passive reception — interact with it, submit to it

Worship God privately and corporately — there is something mystical that happens in corporate worship that does not happen anywhere else; being in the Lord's house on the Lord's Day is how we grow in the Lord
Pray for and with other people — prayer life should reflect the two great commandments: love God and love others; do not spend the whole time praying for yourself
Fellowship with other believers — even a sister going through cancer finds that not being in church because of her chemo schedule is one of the worst parts of her suffering
Participate thoroughly in the sacraments — the Lord's Supper is a means of grace; Westminster Larger Catechism Q.162-167 gives guidance on how to prepare, participate, and reflect afterward

Crucify the flesh — John Owen: if you are not killing sin, sin is killing you; the expulsive power of a new affection (Thomas Chalmers) — if you are in love with Christ, that love will expel the desires of the flesh
Memorize and meditate on Scripture — 52 verses will be sent for the year; thinking on them while driving, walking, etc.

outline.title: 4. The Fruit of the Spirit and Its Relational Impact (vv.22-26, 6:1)
subpoints: "Fruit" is singular — not several independent fruits but one fruit with many facets; all present at different levels in the life of every Christian at all times; same source, same root: walking in the Spirit
Fruit of the Spirit is different from gifts of the Spirit: gifts vary from person to person and from era to era; fruit is for all believers at all times
The fruit and the works of the flesh are antithetical: fornication vs. self-control; outbursts of wrath vs. patience
If you want joy, love, peace — do not pursue joy, love, peace; pursue walking in the Spirit, because they are fruit; cultivate the tree and it will produce the fruit

Relational impact (verses 26 and 6:1): instead of conceit — others-oriented humility; instead of provoking one another — encouragement and edification; instead of envy — rejoicing with those who rejoice and weeping with those who weep; and the ability to help each other overcome sin in gentleness and humility (Galatians 6:1)

summary: The concluding sermon in the Fruit of the Spirit series brings the whole series together by examining the passage as a whole in the context of the grace of God. Pastor Lyro identifies the two great enemies of grace in the Christian life: legalism (earn God's love through rule-keeping) and license (grace means no obligations). Both are naturally attractive to the flesh; both are false. The true alternative the Holy Spirit offers is to walk in the Spirit — a phrase that means at least five things: you have been born of the Spirit, you are controlled by the Spirit, you confess who Jesus is, the fruit of the Spirit is present in your life, and you have a correct view of the law of God. The law must neither be blindly followed nor disregarded; it functions to expose sin and drive us to Christ, after which Christ sends us back to the law as a pattern for demonstrating our love. Paul's list of the works of the flesh is organized into five categories — sexual sins, relationship-destroying sins, God-displacing sins, and bodily sins — with the severe warning that those who practice these things without repentance or struggle will not inherit the kingdom of God. Practical means of walking in the Spirit are laid out: Scripture reading, worship, prayer, fellowship, participation in the sacraments, mortifying the flesh, and memorizing Scripture. The fruit of the Spirit is singular — one fruit with many facets, present in every believer at all times — and it produces a measurable relational impact: humility instead of conceit, encouragement instead of provocation, rejoicing and weeping together, and the ability to restore those who stumble in a spirit of gentleness.
transcript:
## Introduction

On this last Sunday of the year, we bring the series on the fruit of the Spirit to a conclusion. I think it is a good place to end the year and start thinking about the year to come. I want to finish this series by considering the fruit of the Spirit in the context of the grace of God.

This is the 12th or 13th sermon on the fruit of the Spirit. We looked at each one separately; now we conclude by looking at the passage as a whole — at how the fruit of the Spirit relate to the grace of God.

Love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control are the result of the grace of God in our lives. The two great enemies of the grace of God in our lives are legalism and license.

The legalist proclaims: follow Christ by obeying these particular rules, and if you obey them, God will then — and only then — love you. The one who lives according to license rebuts: no, let us sin so that grace may all the more abound — just do what your flesh wants, and the grace of God will be magnified.

Both of these voices are very loud and naturally attractive to our flesh. The idea that we need to first obey in order to be loved — that appeals to our natural heart. The idea that God has no claims on our lives, that he only wants to save us and does not care what we do — that is equally tempting. Both are false. So how does the Christian deal with these loud voices? Paul tells us: the voice to listen to is the voice of the Holy Spirit as he speaks through his word. And what does he say? Walk in the Spirit.

## 1. What It Means to Walk in the Spirit

Verses 16 and 25 tell us to walk in the Spirit. Walking in, walking by, or walking with the Spirit means at least five things.

First: you have received life from the Spirit. Verse 25 says "if you live in the Spirit" — this is a true-to-fact conditional sentence; it is not "if" but "since." Since you live in the Spirit, you have been born again, born from above. Jesus told Nicodemus: you must be born not of the will of man but of the will of God.

Second: you are controlled by the Spirit. Romans 8:1-2 — there is therefore now no condemnation to those who are in Christ Jesus, who do not walk according to the flesh but according to the Spirit. Romans 8:4-5 — those who live according to the Spirit set their minds on the things of the Spirit. Romans 8:9 — if the Spirit of God dwells in you, he controls what you do and think. Romans 8:13 — if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live.

Third: you confess that Jesus is who the Bible says he is. 1 John 4:2 — by this you know the Spirit of God: every spirit that confesses that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is of God.

Fourth: the fruit of the Spirit is present in your life — Galatians 5:22-23.

Fifth: you have a correct view of the law of God. The law of God should neither be blindly followed nor disregarded. Both these positions are often flaunted as spiritual — but they are both missing the Spirit. The law cannot be disregarded because there is no gospel without it. But the law cannot be blindly followed because it is designed to open our eyes to see Christ.

Romans 7: what shall we say — is the law sin? Certainly not. I would not have known sin except through the law. The law of God drives us to Jesus. At the end of Romans 7, Paul cries out: *O wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me from this body of death?* And then: *I thank God — through Jesus Christ our Lord!* The law put him there; then it drove him to Christ.

Once the law drives us to Christ and the Spirit opens our eyes to our need of him, once we trust in him and nothing else for salvation, then Jesus points us back to the law as the pattern to use to demonstrate our love for him. But that follows his love for us; it does not precede. John 14:15 — *if you love me, keep my commandments.* It is not: keep my commandments and then you love me. The love and faith come first; the keeping is following love.

Charles Spurgeon: "What is God's law now? It is not above a Christian; it is under a Christian. The law is the road which guides us, not the rod which drives us, nor the spirit which actuates us. The law is good and excellent, if it keeps its place."

## 2. The Works of the Flesh

Since we live in the Spirit, we walk in the Spirit. But what about legalism and license — how are they defeated?

Legalism is defeated by walking in the Spirit. Verse 18 — if you are led by the Spirit, you are not under law.

License is also defeated by walking in the Spirit. Verses 19-21: *the works of the flesh are evident.* Paul lists them and closes with a severe warning: *those who practice such things will not inherit the kingdom of God.* Notice the seriousness. Those who unrepentantly, consistently, and without any struggle participate in these attitudes and actions are not going to heaven. This is not about those who fall and repent; this is about those for whom these things are a way of life without struggle.

Five categories in this list:

Sexually related sins: adultery; fornication, which is sexual immorality by unmarried people and includes pornography; uncleanness, which is all other sexual immoralities including homosexuality; lewdness, which includes not just the practice but the flaunting and approval of sexual immorality.

Eight relationship-destroying sins: hatred, contentions, jealousies, outbursts of wrath, selfish ambitions, dissensions, heresies (here meaning creating division in the body of Christ to gain control or to ostracize others), envy, murders.

Three God-displacing sins: idolatry; sorcery — the Greek word is pharmakeia; in the ancient world witches prepared and administered deadly poisons and drugs to reach ecstatic states; the postmodern parallel includes abortion and euthanasia (killing by chemical substances) and the use of drugs to reach an altered spiritual state; Ephesians 5:18 says do not get drunk — do not alter your mind to get close to God; be filled with the Spirit instead; and selfish ambition.

Two bodily sins: drunkenness — any recreational altering of the mind; and reveling, which more often relates to overeating.

This list is not exhaustive. Paul adds "and the like." Do not use the list to excuse your sin because it is not listed, or to judge others whose sin appears on it.

The solution for these sins? Repentance — turning to Jesus for forgiveness and grace to overcome. And then, according to our passage, walking in the Spirit — making more room for Jesus.

## 3. Walking in the Spirit Practically

Read the Bible and listen to it preached — with an act of listening, not passive reception. Interact with what is being said, submit to it.

Worship God privately and corporately. There is something mystical — and mystical things are not bad — that happens in corporate worship that does not happen anywhere else. I do not know if it comes across, but we are really eager for people to be in church on the Lord's Day. Not because the elders want to build an empire of high average attendance. The reason we always teach to be in the Lord's house on the Lord's Day is because that is how you grow in the Lord.

Pray for and with other people. Do not spend your whole prayer time praying for yourself. The two great commandments — love God and love others — are all others-oriented. Your prayer life should reflect that.

Fellowship with other believers. I was talking to a sister going through cancer, and she said one of the worst things about it is not being in church because her chemo schedule falls on Sundays. She understands the importance of fellowship with other believers.

Participate thoroughly in the sacraments. We are convinced as a church that the Lord's Supper should take place every Lord's Day. Saints who sin need the grace of God all we can get, and the sacrament is a means of grace. The Westminster Larger Catechism questions 162 to 167 give excellent guidance on how to participate — leading up to it, while taking it, and afterward.

Crucify the flesh. Galatians 5:24 — those who are Christ's have crucified the flesh with its passions and desires. Galatians 2:20 — I have been crucified with Christ. Romans 13:14 — put on the Lord Jesus Christ and make no provision for the flesh to fulfill its lusts. John Owen in *The Mortification of Sin*: if you are not killing sin, sin is killing you.

The expulsive power of a new affection — Thomas Chalmers, whose sermon by that name guided Robert Murray McShane. What motivated Chalmers was his love for Christ that expelled the desires of the flesh. You will only walk in the Spirit if you are in love with Christ, because that love will push all those other things aside.

Memoize and meditate on Scripture. This week I will be sending you 52 verses as suggestions for the year — one per week. Keep the scripture in your hearts and minds and meditate on it throughout the day.

## 4. The Fruit of the Spirit and Its Relational Impact

The result of walking in the Spirit is the fruit of the Spirit — note that it is the fruit, singular. These are not several independent fruits but one fruit with many facets. All are present at different levels in the life of every Christian at all times. There is never a time when love, joy, peace, long-suffering, self-control, patience, goodness, gentleness, and faithfulness should not be present. The fruit of the Spirit is not the same as the gifts of the Spirit: gifts vary from person to person and from era to era; the fruit is for all believers at all times.

The fruit and the works of the flesh are antithetical. Fornication vs. self-control. Outbursts of wrath vs. patience.

And notice: if you want joy, love, and peace in your life, you do not pursue joy, love, and peace directly. Your pursuit is walking in the Spirit, because they are fruit — they are the result. If you want an apple tree to produce apples, you cultivate the tree, and the result is fruit. Do not staple apples to it.

The relational impact of walking in the Spirit, verses 26 and 6:1: instead of conceit — empty glory, vainglory — we have others-oriented humility. Instead of provoking one another — encouragement and edification. Instead of envy — rejoicing with those who rejoice and weeping with those who weep. And the ability in Galatians 6:1 to help one another overcome sin in a spirit of gentleness.

Jesus has invaded our lives with his grace. We have his Spirit working in us. We are branches of the vine producing the singular fruit of love, joy, peace, longsuffering, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control. There is no law against this harvest of spirit-cultivated fruit. And all of this is the result of the power of the grace of God in our lives. Praise God from whom all blessings flow.
